questions.question: What is the 'Latte Factor'?
options: A special coffee shop discount for regular customers
The concept that small daily expenses add up to large annual costs
A budgeting method that only tracks coffee purchases
The ratio of wants to needs in your budget
questions.explanation: The Latte Factor illustrates how small, seemingly insignificant daily expenses (like a daily coffee) can add up to surprisingly large amounts over a year. It's about awareness, not deprivation.

questions.question: The first step to controlling spending is to track where your money actually goes.
questions.explanation: Absolutely! You can't control what you don't measure. Tracking spending for even one month reveals surprising patterns and is the foundation of any budgeting strategy.

questions.question: In the 50/30/20 budget rule, what does the 20% represent?
options: Needs (rent, food, utilities)
Wants (entertainment, dining out)
Savings and extra debt payments
Emergency expenses
questions.explanation: In the 50/30/20 rule: 50% goes to Needs, 30% to Wants, and 20% to Savings & Debt Payoff. The 20% includes emergency fund, investments, and extra debt payments beyond minimums.

questions.question: What is the 'Pay Yourself First' budgeting method?
options: Paying all your bills before spending on wants
Automating savings transfers before spending anything else
Calculating your hourly wage for every purchase
Keeping a portion of your paycheck in cash
questions.explanation: Pay Yourself First means automatically transferring your savings amount (e.g., 20%) to a savings or investment account as soon as you get paid, BEFORE any other spending. This ensures saving happens consistently.

questions.question: What is the '24-Hour Rule' for combating impulse purchases?
options: Only shop for 24 hours per month
Return items within 24 hours for a refund
Wait 24 hours before buying non-essential items
Limit online browsing to 24 minutes per day
questions.explanation: The 24-Hour Rule means waiting a full day before making a non-essential purchase. Most impulse urges fade within this time, helping you avoid regretful spending.

questions.question: Paying with a credit card makes people tend to spend less than paying with cash.
questions.explanation: Research consistently shows the opposite — paying with cards leads to HIGHER spending because it feels less 'real' than handing over physical cash. This is why the envelope system uses cash to control spending.

questions.question: When comparing product prices, what should you look at instead of the total package price?
options: The brand name
The unit price (cost per ounce/unit)
The package size
The shelf placement
questions.explanation: The unit price (cost per ounce, gram, or unit) is the most accurate way to compare products. A larger package isn't always cheaper per unit, and a smaller one might actually be the better deal.

questions.question: You should audit your subscriptions at least every 3 months to find unused services.
questions.explanation: Regular subscription audits help you catch services you've stopped using. The average person pays for subscriptions they've forgotten about, and a quarterly review can save hundreds of dollars per year.
